title: Negation, deepened
description: A2 expansion of negation beyond ne...pas: ne...plus, ne...jamais, ne...rien, ne...personne, and ne...que. Placement rules in simple tenses and the passé composé. Explained in English.

cards.notes: Use **ne ... plus** to say something that used to be true is no longer true. *Je ne travaille **plus** ici.* (I no longer work here.) In the passé composé the **plus** goes between the auxiliary and the participle: *Je **n'ai plus** mangé de viande.* (I no longer ate meat.)

**ne ... jamais** = never. *Je ne mens **jamais**.* (I never lie.) In the passé composé: *Je **n'ai jamais** vu la mer.* (I have never seen the sea.) Note: *jamais* can also mean 'ever' in questions — *As-tu **jamais** voyagé?* — but in a negation frame it always means 'never'.

**ne ... rien** = nothing. *Je ne dis **rien**.* (I say nothing.) In the passé composé, **rien** sits between the auxiliary and the participle, just like pas: *Je **n'ai rien** dit.* (I said nothing.) **rien** can also act as the **subject**: *Rien ne marche.* (Nothing works.) — here **ne** comes right after *rien*.

**ne ... personne** = nobody. Unlike the others, in the passé composé **personne** comes **after** the past participle: *Je **n'ai vu personne**.* (I saw nobody.) This is the key exception to learn. **personne** can also be the **subject**: *Personne ne vient.* (Nobody is coming.)
cards.hint: personne is the odd one out: it follows the participle in the passe compose.

cards.notes: **ne ... que** does not negate — it **restricts**: *Je ne bois **qu'**eau.* (I drink only water.) The **que** goes directly before the restricted element (not necessarily at the end). In the passé composé: *Il **n'a** mangé **que** du pain.* (He only ate bread.) This is sometimes called *restriction* rather than negation.

cards.front: Je n'ai jamais mangé... / Je n'ai rien dit. / Je n'ai vu personne.
cards.back: negation placement in the passe compose
cards.notes:
In the passé composé, most negatives sit **between the auxiliary and the participle** (like *pas*): *ne + auxiliary + **plus/jamais/rien** + participle*. **personne** is the exception — it goes **after** the participle: *Je n'ai vu **personne**.*

| Negative | Position in passe compose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **ne...pas** | aux | *n'ai **pas** mangé* |
| **ne...plus** | aux | *n'ai **plus** mangé* |
| **ne...jamais** | aux | *n'ai **jamais** mangé* |
| **ne...rien** | aux | *n'ai **rien** dit* |
| **ne...personne** | after participle | *n'ai vu **personne*** |

cards.front: Personne ne vient. / Rien ne marche.
cards.notes: **rien** and **personne** can be the **subject** of a sentence. In that position, they come **first**, followed immediately by **ne** and then the verb. *Personne ne vient.* (Nobody is coming.) *Rien ne marche.* (Nothing works.) The verb stays in 3rd-person singular.

cards.front: Je sais pas. (spoken) vs. Je ne sais pas. (written)
cards.back: the ne is often dropped in casual spoken French
cards.notes: In everyday spoken French the **ne** is frequently omitted: *Je sais pas, J'ai jamais vu ca.* However, in **writing** and **formal speech** the *ne* is required. At A2 level you should always write and produce the full form with *ne*.
cards.hint: Always keep the ne in writing.

cards.front: Je n'ai vu personne. (NOT: Je n'ai pas vu personne.)
cards.back: French uses ONE negative word with ne — not two
cards.notes: English speakers sometimes write *Je n'ai **pas** vu personne* — a double negative that calques 'I didn't see nobody.' In French, **ne** + **one** negative word is the correct pattern. Adding **pas** alongside another negative (jamais, rien, personne) is an error: *ne **pas** jamais, ne **pas** rien* are wrong.
*ne ... plus* in a simple tense: *ne* before the verb, *plus* after it. Compare: *Il ne travaille **pas*** (he does not work) vs. *Il ne travaille **plus*** (he no longer works — he used to).
Passé composé with **ne...jamais**: *ne* before the auxiliary, *jamais* between auxiliary and participle.
**ne...rien** in a simple present tense: *ne* before the verb, *rien* after it.
**ne...personne** in the passé composé: *personne* comes **after** the past participle *invité*. This is the key exception.
**ne...que**: *que* (shortened to *qu'* before a vowel) sits directly before the restricted element (*une heure*). This expresses restriction, not absence.

steps.title: Beyond ne...pas — the five negative constructions
steps.body:
# Negation, deepened

In A1 you learned **ne ... pas**. French has four more core negative constructions — each slots into the same position around the verb:

| French | English meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **ne ... pas** | not | *Je ne mange **pas** de viande.* |
| **ne ... plus** | not anymore / no longer | *Je ne travaille **plus** ici.* |
| **ne ... jamais** | never | *Il ne ment **jamais**.* |
| **ne ... rien** | nothing / not anything | *Elle ne dit **rien**.* |
| **ne ... personne** | nobody / not anybody | *Je ne vois **personne**.* |
| **ne ... que** | only (restriction) | *Je bois **que** de l'eau.* |

## The pattern

In a simple tense (present, imparfait), the structure is:

> **ne** + VERB + **negative word**

So: *Je ne mange **rien**.* / *Tu ne lis **jamais**.* / *Elle ne vient **plus**.*

## Important note about spoken French

In **casual speech** the *ne* is very often dropped: *Je sais pas, J'ai jamais vu ca.* In **writing** and **formal French** the *ne* is always required. This course always uses the full written form.

steps.title: Placement in the passe compose
steps.body:
## Where does the negative word go in the passe compose?

In the passé composé you have two verb parts: **auxiliary** (avoir/etre) + **past participle**. Most negative words sit **between** them — exactly where *pas* goes:

> **ne** + AUXILIARY + **negative word** + PARTICIPLE

| Construction | Example | English |
|---|---|---|
| ne ... pas | *Je **n'ai pas** mangé.* | I did not eat. |
| ne ... plus | *Je **n'ai plus** mangé de viande.* | I no longer ate meat. |
| ne ... jamais | *Il **n'a jamais** vu la mer.* | He has never seen the sea. |
| ne ... rien | *Elle **n'a rien** dit.* | She said nothing. |

## The one exception — personne

**ne...personne** is different: **personne** comes **after** the past participle:

> **ne** + AUXILIARY + PARTICIPLE + **personne**

*Je **n'ai vu personne**.* (I saw nobody.) NOT: *Je n'ai personne vu.*

Memory tip: *personne* is a full noun-like word and takes the same slot as a noun object — which follows the participle.

steps.title: Rien and personne as subjects; ne...que
steps.body:
## Rien and personne as subjects

**rien** and **personne** can start a sentence as the **subject**. In that case, they come first and **ne** follows immediately:

> **Rien / Personne** + **ne** + VERB

| French | English |
|---|---|
| *Rien ne marche.* | Nothing works. |
| *Personne ne vient.* | Nobody is coming. |
| *Rien n'a change.* | Nothing changed. |
| *Personne n'a repondu.* | Nobody replied. |

The verb is always **3rd-person singular**.

## ne...que — restriction, not negation

**ne...que** does not truly negate — it **restricts** to one element (like English 'only'):

*Il **n'a** mange **que** du pain.* — He only ate bread. (not: he ate nothing)

**que** goes directly before the restricted word or phrase. In the passé composé the structure is: *ne + auxiliary + participle + que + restricted element*.

## The double-negative trap

English sometimes uses double negatives ('I didn't see nobody'). French **never** does. Use **ne + one negative word** only:

- Correct: *Je **n'ai vu personne**.* (I saw nobody.)
- Wrong: *Je **n'ai pas vu personne**.* (mixing pas + personne)

steps.title: Match the negation to its meaning

exercise.prompt: Match each French negative construction to its English meaning.

steps.title: Translate: I have never eaten sushi.

exercise.prompt: Translate: **I have never eaten sushi.** (passe compose — jamais goes between auxiliary and participle)
accept: Je n'ai jamais mange de sushi.
Je n'ai jamais mange du sushi.
Je n'ai jamais mange de sushis.
je n'ai jamais mange de sushi.
distractors: Je n'ai pas jamais mange de sushi.
Je n'ai mange jamais de sushi.
Je n'ai pas mange de sushi jamais.
Je jamais ai mange de sushi.

exercise.prompt: Complete the sentence with the correct negative word meaning 'no longer'.
exercise.sentence: Il ne travaille ___ dans ce bureau.

exercise.prompt: Translate: **She said nothing.** (passe compose — rien sits between auxiliary and participle)
exercise.hint: ne + n'a + rien + dit. Rien stays between auxiliary and participle.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words to say **I saw nobody.** (passe compose — personne goes AFTER the participle)
exercise.hint: personne is the exception: ne + auxiliary + participle + personne.

exercise.prompt: A woman says 'I have never visited Paris.' Which French sentence is correct?

images.label: Je n'ai pas jamais visite Paris.

exercise.hint: jamais goes between auxiliary and participle; ne is required in writing; never mix pas with jamais.
distractors: Je n'ai pas jamais visite Paris.

exercise.prompt: Translate: **Nobody is coming.** (personne as subject — use present tense)
exercise.hint: personne as subject: Personne + ne + verb. Verb is 3rd-person singular.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the blank to express restriction: 'He only ate bread.' (passe compose)
exercise.hint: ne ... que = only; que goes directly before the restricted element.

exercise.prompt: Match each French sentence (passe compose) to its English translation.
Arrange the words to say **Nothing works.** (rien as subject)
exercise.hint: rien as subject: Rien + ne + verb (3rd-person singular).

steps.title: Translate: We only have one hour.

exercise.prompt: Translate: **We only have one hour.** (use ne...que; present tense: nous avons)
exercise.hint: ne ... que: Nous n'avons + que (contracts to qu' before vowel) + une heure.
distractors: Nous n'avons que pas une heure.
